{{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55528104]]
Wikidata name: Sekison Gongen
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Sekison Gongen''' (石尊権現) is a deity of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] that emerged from the fusion of the [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain Worship]] and [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]-oriented beliefs of [[:en:Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|Oyama]], and enshrines [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]]. It is often misunderstood because the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} was [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], but the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of the Kannon-ji in {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} is the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]].<ref name="#1">Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Tenpō 6)</ref> Before the implementation of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]], it was enshrined at Isurugi shrines nationwide after being invited from [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]] and {{ill|Amefuriyama Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Amefuriyama Daisanji|lt_ja=雨降山大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amefuriyama Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} via [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]. It is also known as '''Sekison Daigongen''' or '''Oyama Sekison Daigongen'''.

== Overview ==
In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. Within Oyama, when the once forbidden inner area was developed as a training ground for ancient Buddhist ascetics and Daisanji was established, the stone seat at the summit and the double waterfall came to be regarded as important sacred sites within Daisanji. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. The honji Buddha of Sekison Gongen was the Eleven-Faced Kannon.<ref name="#1"/> In the area where the current lower shrine of Afuri Shrine is located, there once stood the Fudō-dō, which served as the main hall of Daisanji. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai School]] and the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon School]]. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Koyasan Shingon School]], yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.

During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ([[:en:Kō (lecture)|Oyama Kō]]) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]’s prayers for victory — became popular. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ([[:en:Priest|Guiding Priest]]) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.

== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku ==
Due to the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s [[:en:Ōyama Cable Car|Daisanji Station]], thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.

== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s [[:en:Ōyama Cable Car|Daisanji Station]], thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55528104]] Wikidata name: Sekison Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sekison Gongen''' (石尊権現) is a deity of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] that emerged from the fusion of the [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain Worship]] and [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]-oriented beliefs of [[:en:Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|Oyama]], and enshrines [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]]. It is often misunderstood because the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} was [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], but the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of the Kannon-ji in {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} is the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]].<ref name="#1">Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Tenpō 6)</ref> Before the implementation of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]], it was enshrined at Isurugi shrines nationwide after being invited from [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]] and {{ill|Amefuriyama Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Amefuriyama Daisanji|lt_ja=雨降山大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amefuriyama Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} via [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]. It is also known as '''Sekison Daigongen''' or '''Oyama Sekison Daigongen'''.
== Overview == In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. Within Oyama, when the once forbidden inner area was developed as a training ground for ancient Buddhist ascetics and Daisanji was established, the stone seat at the summit and the double waterfall came to be regarded as important sacred sites within Daisanji. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. The honji Buddha of Sekison Gongen was the Eleven-Faced Kannon.<ref name="#1"/> In the area where the current lower shrine of Afuri Shrine is located, there once stood the Fudō-dō, which served as the main hall of Daisanji. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai School]] and the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon School]]. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Koyasan Shingon School]], yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.
== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ([[:en:Kō (lecture)|Oyama Kō]]) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]’s prayers for victory — became popular. This scene is also depicted in [[:en:Ukiyo-e|Ukiyo-e]], which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Representative examples include works such as “Daitō Daigan Jōju Aru ga Taki Tsubo” by [[:en:Utagawa Toyokuni|Utagawa Toyokuni]] and “Oyama Sekison Daigongen” by [[:en:Utagawa Yoshitora|Utagawa Yoshitora]]. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ([[:en:Priest|Guiding Priest]]) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s [[:en:Ōyama Cable Car|Daisanji Station]], thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.

It is often misunderstood because the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} was [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], but the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of the Kannon-ji in {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} is the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]].<ref name="#1">Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Tenpō 6)</ref> Before the implementation of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]], it was enshrined at Isurugi shrines nationwide after being invited from [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]] and {{ill|Amefuriyama Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Amefuriyama Daisanji|lt_ja=雨降山大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amefuriyama Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} via [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]. It is also known as '''Sekison Daigongen''' or '''Oyama Sekison Daigongen'''.
== Overview == In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. Within Oyama, when the once forbidden inner area was developed as a training ground for ancient Buddhist ascetics and Daisanji was established, the stone seat at the summit and the double waterfall came to be regarded as important sacred sites within Daisanji. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. The honji Buddha of Sekison Gongen was the Eleven-Faced Kannon.<ref name="#1"/> In the area where the current lower shrine of Afuri Shrine is located, there once stood the Fudō-dō, which served as the main hall of Daisanji. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai School]] and the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon School]]. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Koyasan Shingon School]], yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.
== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ([[:en:Kō (lecture)|Oyama Kō]]) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]’s prayers for victory — became popular. This scene is also depicted in [[:en:Ukiyo-e|Ukiyo-e]], which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Representative examples include works such as “Daitō Daigan Jōju Aru ga Taki Tsubo” by [[:en:Utagawa Toyokuni|Utagawa Toyokuni]] and “Oyama Sekison Daigongen” by [[:en:Utagawa Yoshitora|Utagawa Yoshitora]]. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ([[:en:Priest|Guiding Priest]]) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s [[:en:Ōyama Cable Car|Daisanji Station]], thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.

== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai School]] and the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon School]]. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Koyasan Shingon School]], yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.
== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ([[:en:Kō (lecture)|Oyama Kō]]) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]’s prayers for victory — became popular. This scene is also depicted in [[:en:Ukiyo-e|Ukiyo-e]], which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Representative examples include works such as “Daitō Daigan Jōju Aru ga Taki Tsubo” by [[:en:Utagawa Toyokuni|Utagawa Toyokuni]] and “Oyama Sekison Daigongen” by [[:en:Utagawa Yoshitora|Utagawa Yoshitora]]. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ([[:en:Priest|Guiding Priest]]) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s [[:en:Ōyama Cable Car|Daisanji Station]], thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55528104]] Wikidata name: Sekison Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sekison Gongen''' (石尊権現) is a deity of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] that emerged from the fusion of the [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain Worship]] and [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]-oriented beliefs of [[:en:Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|Oyama]], and enshrines [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]]. It is often misunderstood because the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} was [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], but the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of the Kannon-ji in {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} is the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]].<ref name="#1">Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Tenpō 6)</ref> Before the implementation of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]], it was enshrined at Isurugi shrines nationwide after being invited from [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]] and {{ill|Amefuriyama Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Amefuriyama Daisanji|lt_ja=雨降山大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amefuriyama Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} via [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]. It is also known as '''Sekison Daigongen''' or '''Oyama Sekison Daigongen'''.
== Overview == In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. Within Oyama, when the once forbidden inner area was developed as a training ground for ancient Buddhist ascetics and Daisanji was established, the stone seat at the summit and the double waterfall came to be regarded as important sacred sites within Daisanji. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. The honji Buddha of Sekison Gongen was the Eleven-Faced Kannon.<ref name="#1"/> In the area where the current lower shrine of Afuri Shrine is located, there once stood the Fudō-dō, which served as the main hall of Daisanji. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai School]] and the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon School]]. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Koyasan Shingon School]], yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.
== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ([[:en:Kō (lecture)|Oyama Kō]]) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]’s prayers for victory — became popular. This scene is also depicted in [[:en:Ukiyo-e|Ukiyo-e]], which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Representative examples include works such as “Daitō Daigan Jōju Aru ga Taki Tsubo” by [[:en:Utagawa Toyokuni|Utagawa Toyokuni]] and “Oyama Sekison Daigongen” by [[:en:Utagawa Yoshitora|Utagawa Yoshitora]]. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ([[:en:Priest|Guiding Priest]]) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s [[:en:Ōyama Cable Car|Daisanji Station]], thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.

Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55528104]] Wikidata name: Sekison Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sekison Gongen''' (石尊権現) is a deity of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] that emerged from the fusion of the [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain Worship]] and [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]-oriented beliefs of [[:en:Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|Oyama]], and enshrines [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]]. It is often misunderstood because the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} was [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], but the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of the Kannon-ji in {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} is the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]].<ref name="#1">Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Tenpō 6)</ref> Before the implementation of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]], it was enshrined at Isurugi shrines nationwide after being invited from [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]] and {{ill|Amefuriyama Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Amefuriyama Daisanji|lt_ja=雨降山大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amefuriyama Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} via [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]. It is also known as '''Sekison Daigongen''' or '''Oyama Sekison Daigongen'''.
== Overview == In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. Within Oyama, when the once forbidden inner area was developed as a training ground for ancient Buddhist ascetics and Daisanji was established, the stone seat at the summit and the double waterfall came to be regarded as important sacred sites within Daisanji. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. The honji Buddha of Sekison Gongen was the Eleven-Faced Kannon.<ref name="#1"/> In the area where the current lower shrine of Afuri Shrine is located, there once stood the Fudō-dō, which served as the main hall of Daisanji. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai School]] and the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon School]]. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Koyasan Shingon School]], yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.
== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ([[:en:Kō (lecture)|Oyama Kō]]) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]’s prayers for victory — became popular. This scene is also depicted in [[:en:Ukiyo-e|Ukiyo-e]], which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Representative examples include works such as “Daitō Daigan Jōju Aru ga Taki Tsubo” by [[:en:Utagawa Toyokuni|Utagawa Toyokuni]] and “Oyama Sekison Daigongen” by [[:en:Utagawa Yoshitora|Utagawa Yoshitora]]. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ([[:en:Priest|Guiding Priest]]) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s [[:en:Ōyama Cable Car|Daisanji Station]], thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|Oyama]] * {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} * {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}}
{{translated page|ja|石尊権現|version=102194969|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

It is often misunderstood because the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} was [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], but the [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] of the Kannon-ji in {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} is the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]].<ref name="#1">Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Tenpō 6)</ref> Before the implementation of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]], it was enshrined at Isurugi shrines nationwide after being invited from [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]] and {{ill|Amefuriyama Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Amefuriyama Daisanji|lt_ja=雨降山大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amefuriyama Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} via [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]. It is also known as '''Sekison Daigongen''' or '''Oyama Sekison Daigongen'''.
== Overview == In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. Within Oyama, when the once forbidden inner area was developed as a training ground for ancient Buddhist ascetics and Daisanji was established, the stone seat at the summit and the double waterfall came to be regarded as important sacred sites within Daisanji. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. The honji Buddha of Sekison Gongen was the Eleven-Faced Kannon.<ref name="#1"/> In the area where the current lower shrine of Afuri Shrine is located, there once stood the Fudō-dō, which served as the main hall of Daisanji. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai School]] and the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon School]]. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Koyasan Shingon School]], yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.
== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ([[:en:Kō (lecture)|Oyama Kō]]) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]’s prayers for victory — became popular. This scene is also depicted in [[:en:Ukiyo-e|Ukiyo-e]], which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Representative examples include works such as “Daitō Daigan Jōju Aru ga Taki Tsubo” by [[:en:Utagawa Toyokuni|Utagawa Toyokuni]] and “Oyama Sekison Daigongen” by [[:en:Utagawa Yoshitora|Utagawa Yoshitora]]. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ([[:en:Priest|Guiding Priest]]) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s [[:en:Ōyama Cable Car|Daisanji Station]], thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|Oyama]] * {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} * {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55528104]] Wikidata name: Sekison Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sekison Gongen''' (石尊権現) is a deity of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} that emerged from the fusion of the {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain Worship}} and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō}}-oriented beliefs of {{ill|Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|en|Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|WD=Q386518|lt=Oyama}}, and enshrines {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon}} as its {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha}}. It is often misunderstood because the {{ill|Honzon|en|Honzon|WD=Q11520160|lt=Honzon}} of {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} was {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}, but the {{ill|Honzon|en|Honzon|WD=Q11520160|lt=Honzon}} of the Kannon-ji in {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} is the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha}}.<ref name="#1">Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Tenpō 6)</ref> Before the implementation of {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}, it was enshrined at Isurugi shrines nationwide after being invited from {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province}} and {{ill|Amefuriyama Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Amefuriyama Daisanji|lt_ja=雨降山大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amefuriyama Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} via {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}}. It is also known as '''Sekison Daigongen''' or '''Oyama Sekison Daigongen'''.
== Overview == In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. Within Oyama, when the once forbidden inner area was developed as a training ground for ancient Buddhist ascetics and Daisanji was established, the stone seat at the summit and the double waterfall came to be regarded as important sacred sites within Daisanji. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. The honji Buddha of Sekison Gongen was the Eleven-Faced Kannon.<ref name="#1"/> In the area where the current lower shrine of Afuri Shrine is located, there once stood the Fudō-dō, which served as the main hall of Daisanji. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai School}} and the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon School}}. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|en|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357|lt=Koyasan Shingon School}}, yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.
== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ({{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|WD=Q11632184|lt=Oyama Kō}}) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}’s prayers for victory — became popular. This scene is also depicted in {{ill|Ukiyo-e|en|Ukiyo-e|WD=Q185905|lt=Ukiyo-e}}, which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Representative examples include works such as “Daitō Daigan Jōju Aru ga Taki Tsubo” by {{ill|Utagawa Toyokuni|en|Utagawa Toyokuni|WD=Q950316|lt=Utagawa Toyokuni}} and “Oyama Sekison Daigongen” by {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitora|en|Utagawa Yoshitora|WD=Q3075041|lt=Utagawa Yoshitora}}. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ({{ill|Priest|en|Priest|WD=Q42603|lt=Guiding Priest}}) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s {{ill|Ōyama Cable Car|en|Ōyama Cable Car|WD=Q864604|lt=Daisanji Station}}, thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.

It is often misunderstood because the {{ill|Honzon|en|Honzon|WD=Q11520160|lt=Honzon}} of {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} was {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}, but the {{ill|Honzon|en|Honzon|WD=Q11520160|lt=Honzon}} of the Kannon-ji in {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} is the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha}}.<ref name="#1">Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Tenpō 6)</ref> Before the implementation of {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}, it was enshrined at Isurugi shrines nationwide after being invited from {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province}} and {{ill|Amefuriyama Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Amefuriyama Daisanji|lt_ja=雨降山大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amefuriyama Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}} via {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}}. It is also known as '''Sekison Daigongen''' or '''Oyama Sekison Daigongen'''.
== Overview == In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. Within Oyama, when the once forbidden inner area was developed as a training ground for ancient Buddhist ascetics and Daisanji was established, the stone seat at the summit and the double waterfall came to be regarded as important sacred sites within Daisanji. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. The honji Buddha of Sekison Gongen was the Eleven-Faced Kannon.<ref name="#1"/> In the area where the current lower shrine of Afuri Shrine is located, there once stood the Fudō-dō, which served as the main hall of Daisanji. It is believed that during the medieval period, Daisanji—as a mountain temple organized as a single-mountain institution under its betto—comprised various members, including lay followers, centered on religious practitioners of both the {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai School}} and the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon School}}. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|en|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357|lt=Koyasan Shingon School}}, yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. The Sekison Gongen festival, which had been observed continuously from ancient through medieval times, persisted into the Edo period, and the faith in Sekison Gongen is recorded in works such as the {{ill|Daisanji Engi Emaki|ja|大山寺縁起 (相模国)|lt=Daisanji Engi Emaki|lt_ja=大山寺縁起絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji Engi Emaki|1=Daisanji Engi Emaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97184205}} and the Daisan Fudō Reigenki.
== Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. Throughout the Kantō region, Oyama associations ({{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|WD=Q11632184|lt=Oyama Kō}}) were organized, and the sword offering known as “Nasaetachi” — originating from the legend of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}’s prayers for victory — became popular. This scene is also depicted in {{ill|Ukiyo-e|en|Ukiyo-e|WD=Q185905|lt=Ukiyo-e}}, which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Representative examples include works such as “Daitō Daigan Jōju Aru ga Taki Tsubo” by {{ill|Utagawa Toyokuni|en|Utagawa Toyokuni|WD=Q950316|lt=Utagawa Toyokuni}} and “Oyama Sekison Daigongen” by {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitora|en|Utagawa Yoshitora|WD=Q3075041|lt=Utagawa Yoshitora}}. As the Oyama pilgrimage grew in popularity, lodgings for pilgrims (shukubō) were established in rows, and guiding priests ({{ill|Priest|en|Priest|WD=Q42603|lt=Guiding Priest}}) became active. The scale of the pilgrimage reached such proportions that there were as many as eight major temples, eighteen lodges, and over 150 priests.<ref>Akizato Rojima, “Tokaidō Meishō Zukkai” (Kansei 9)</ref> Moreover, the Sekison Gongen faith spread throughout the Kantō region, and in various locales, names such as “Sekison-yama” and the establishment of Sekison shrines emerged, where Sekison associations were also formed and sword offerings were dedicated. The Oyama pilgrimage associations, centered in Sagami and Musashi, extended to Anba, Shimōsa, Jōsō, Hitachi, Shimotsuke, Kamitsuke, Iwaki, Kai, Shinano, Echigo, Tōtōmi, Suruga, and Izu, with a total of 15,700 associations and approximately 700,000 households as adherents.<ref>Daisan Kaidōki (Early Meiji)</ref> Pilgrims chanted phrases such as “Zange zange, Rokkon Seijō, Ōmine Hachidai Kongō Dōji, Oyama Daiseid Fudō Myōō, Namu Sekison Daigongen, Daitengu, Shōtengu, Aiken Nōju, Ichiryū Reihai, Kimyō Chōrei” among others.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku == Due to the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} implemented under the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}} of the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the Shinbutsu-shūgō faith in Sekison Gongen was abolished. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the summit Isurugi-sha and Daisanji were reorganized respectively as the summit main shrine (along with the Okusha and Zensha) and the lower shrine of Afuri Shrine. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s {{ill|Ōyama Cable Car|en|Ōyama Cable Car|WD=Q864604|lt=Daisanji Station}}, thus avoiding complete temple abandonment. Furthermore, the Fudō Myōō statue, the honzon of Daisanji, was preserved from Haibutsu Kishaku and continues to be enshrined as the honzon of Daisanji. Additionally, in various locations, some Isurugi shrines survived Haibutsu Kishaku and remain as Sekison shrines.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|en|Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|WD=Q386518|lt=Oyama}} * {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} * {{ill|Daisanji|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|lt=Daisanji|lt_ja=大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisanji|1=Ōyama-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434610}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石尊権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55528104]] Wikidata name: Sekison Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sekison Gongen''' (石尊権現) is a deity of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} that emerged from the fusion of the {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain Worship}} and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō}}-oriented beliefs of {{ill|Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|en|Mount Ōyama (Kanagawa)|WD=Q386518|lt=Oyama}}, and enshrines {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon}} as its {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha}}. == Overview == In Sagami Oyama, based on the ancient plain-region mountain worship described in the {{ill|Engi Shikishinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi Shikishinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, there existed an institution—namely, {{ill|Afuri Shrine|ja|大山阿夫利神社|lt=Afuri Shrine|lt_ja=阿夫利神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afuri Shrine|1=Ōyama Afuri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434713}} (Afuri no Kamino Yashiro)—and it is believed that a shrine enshrining Oyama itself as “Afuri no Kami” stood at the foothills. The stone seat on the summit became the principal object of veneration known as “Sekison Gongen” at the main shrine of Daisanji (the Isurugi-sha),<ref>{{Cite book|title=Daisan Chishi Chōsho (Upper Volume)|date=Tenpō 6|year=Tenpō 6}}</ref> and in addition to Sekison Gongen, deities such as the Daitengu Shrine (present-day Okusha), the Shōtengu Shrine (present-day Zensha), and Tokuichinomiya (present-day Jichininushi) were also enshrined and venerated. In the Edo period, due to the shogunate’s religious policies, the inner area of the mountain was unified under the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|en|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357|lt=Koyasan Shingon School}}, yet temples of various sects continued to exist in the temple town of the Daisanji territory. == Oyama Pilgrimage == During the Edo period, Oyama, along with {{ill|Enoshima|ja|江ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoshima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江の島|1=Enoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q989803}}, became a semi-touristic destination in the vicinity of Edo, and the Oyama pilgrimage flourished. This scene is also depicted in {{ill|Ukiyo-e|en|Ukiyo-e|WD=Q185905|lt=Ukiyo-e}}, which shows pilgrims from Edo carrying large wooden swords inscribed with the name of Sekison Gongen on their way to Oyama. Although many temple treasures were destroyed, the Amefuri-yama Daisanji was rebuilt near the current {{ill|Oyama Cable|ja|大山ケーブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Cable|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大山観光電鉄大山鋼索線|1=Ōyama Cable Car|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864604}}’s {{ill|Ōyama Cable Car|en|Ōyama Cable Car|WD=Q864604|lt=Daisanji Station}}, thus avoiding complete temple abandonment.
